title: Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of 2025
short_title: Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill tells the Government Accountability Office to study how well the Department of Veterans Affairs takes care of women veterans going through menopause and related health issues. The GAO must write a public report within eighteen months that looks at the current care, training, and access to treatment. After getting this report, the Department of Veterans Affairs must create a plan to follow the recommendations and make menopause care better for women veterans. The goal is to improve both the quality of care and how easy it is for veterans to get the help they need.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Comptroller General Study and Report on Menopause Care Furnished by Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text: The Comptroller General of the United States (the chief government watchdog who reviews how federal agencies spend money and provide services) must do a study. This study will look at the medical care the Department of Veterans Affairs gives to women veterans who are going through perimenopause (the time before menopause when periods start changing), genitourinary syndrome of menopause (problems with the bladder and genital area during menopause), and menopause (when periods stop permanently). The Comptroller General must make a public report about this study within eighteen months after this law is passed. The report must include seven main parts. First, it must describe what menopause care the Department of Veterans Affairs currently provides. Second, it must review the Department's rules and procedures for training doctors, diagnosing and treating these menopause conditions, and sending veterans to doctors outside the Department when needed. Third, it must check if veterans can get good access to team-based care from different types of medical specialists to treat menopause effects. Fourth, it must review how the Department teaches veterans about the menopause care they can get, including treatment choices and possible benefits and risks. Fifth, it must judge how good the menopause care is by looking at what veterans say about their care, how well the treatments work, and whether veterans have ways to complain and get problems fixed. Sixth, it must review how the Department uses research from inside or outside the Department to make menopause care better, including developing new treatments. Seventh, it must give suggestions for how to improve menopause care for veterans. After the Comptroller General makes the report public, the Secretary of Veterans Affairs has six months to give Congress a plan. This plan must show how the Department will follow the report's suggestions, make menopause care better, and help veterans get easier access to menopause care.
